<Summary source="https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fda-fully-releases-clinical-hold-intellia-crispr-gene-therapy-trials">
## Summary: FDA Lifts Clinical Hold on Intellia CRISPR Gene Therapy Trials (Fierce Biotech, March 2, 2026)

### Key Facts

- The FDA has **fully lifted clinical holds** on both of Intellia Therapeutics' phase 3 CRISPR gene therapy trials (Magnitude and Magnitude-2), which had been paused since October 2025 following a serious liver toxicity event that resulted in a patient death.
- **Magnitude** tests nexiguran ziclumeran (nex-z) in **transthyretin amyloidosis with cardiomyopathy (ATTR-CM)**; **Magnitude-2** tests it in **hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis with polyneuropathy (ATTRv-PN)** — both conditions distinct from sickle cell disease and beta thalassemia.
- The hold on Magnitude-2 was lifted in **January 2026**; the Magnitude hold was lifted **March 2, 2026**.

### Safety Conditions Attached to Lifted Hold
- Exclusion of patients with certain liver issues, low heart ejection fraction (<25%), or recent cardiovascular instability.
- Enhanced liver enzyme monitoring and steroid treatment guidance for both trials.

### Named Source Opinions
- **Evercore ISI analysts** characterized the safety adjustments as "modest," noting minimal disruption to clinical timelines, but flagged that the **cause of liver toxicity remains unknown**, potentially leading to a "wait and see" approach from doctors and patients even if the drug succeeds.
- **Intellia CEO John Leonard** expressed satisfaction with the FDA alignment and indicated focus will shift to completing enrollment in both trials.

### Broader Context
- Nex-z is a **one-time CRISPR-based treatment** delivered via lipid nanoparticles, targeting the transthyretin gene.
- Liver toxicity is noted as a **recurring challenge** across gene therapies broadly (also flagged in uniQure and Sarepta cases).
- These trials are still in **phase 3** — no approval has been granted yet.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.biopharmadive.com/news/intellia-fda-clinical-hold-crispr-nexiguran-ziclumeran-ttr-amyloidosis/804229/">
## Summary: FDA Places Formal Hold on Two Intellia CRISPR Trials

**Source:** BioPharma Dive | **Date:** October 30, 2025 | **Author:** Delilah Alvarado

### Key Facts:

- The FDA formally placed a **clinical hold** on two Phase 3 trials of Intellia Therapeutics' CRISPR therapy **nexiguran ziclumeran (nex-z)**, compounding a voluntary halt Intellia had already initiated days earlier.
- The trials target **transthyretin amyloidosis** (ATTR), a rare disease where misfolded proteins accumulate and cause organ damage — representing a **new condition** beyond the already-approved sickle cell/beta thalassemia indications.
- The hold was triggered by a **"grade 4" adverse event** (severe spike in liver enzymes and bilirubin) requiring patient hospitalization. A prior grade 4 event had occurred in testing but had not led to hospitalization.
- Over **650 patients** had been enrolled in the cardiomyopathy trial and **47** in the polyneuropathy trial before the stoppage.

### Analyst Opinions (Named Sources):
- **William Blair analyst Myles Minter** noted the FDA hold represents "a higher level of scrutiny than a sponsor electing to recommence a study on its own accord."
- **Leerink Partners' Mani Foroohar** said the hold "further dims" investor sentiment and likely delays a study readout by **"several quarters."**

### Company Response:
- Intellia stated it intends to address the clinical hold "as expeditiously as possible."
- Company shares fell **5% Thursday** and lost **more than half their value** over the week.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://ts2.tech/en/crispr-therapeutics-stock-whipsaws-again-as-takeover-talk-resurfaces-heres-what-to-watch/">
## Summary of Article: "CRISPR Therapeutics stock whipsaws again as takeover talk resurfaces"

**Source:** TechStock² | **Date:** February 27, 2026 | **Author:** Michał Rogucki

---

### Key Facts & Statistics

- **CRISPR Therapeutics stock** slipped ~1.9% to $60.55 in premarket trading on Feb 27, following an **11.85% surge** the prior session (Thursday)
- Thursday's rally was driven by **renewed takeover rumors** surfacing on Betaville's M&A blog (run by Ben Harrington)
- **Casgevy** (CRISPR's approved therapy) generated **$54 million in Q4 revenue** and **$116 million in full-year 2025 revenue**
- CRISPR held **$1.98 billion in cash, equivalents, and marketable securities** at end of 2025
- Vertex Pharmaceuticals holds primary responsibility for global development, manufacturing, and commercialization of Casgevy, with a **60/40 cost/profit split**

### Relevant Context for Gene Editing Approvals

- Casgevy is described as an **ex vivo therapy** (cells edited outside the body and reinfused), approved for sickle cell disease/beta thalassemia — **no new condition approvals mentioned**
- The article frames CRISPR Therapeutics as a "stand-in for the entire first crop of CRISPR/Cas9 drugs," navigating real-world commercialization challenges
- **No pipeline candidates** with imminent regulatory approvals for new conditions are specifically highlighted

### Upcoming Events (Named Sources)
- CEO **Samarth Kulkarni** described "steady progress across a broad and increasingly mature pipeline"
- Management scheduled at **TD Cowen healthcare conference (March 2)** and **Leerink conference (March 11)**

---

**Relevance to Forecast Question:** The article contains no information suggesting an imminent FDA/MHRA/EU approval of a gene editing therapy for a **new condition** beyond sickle cell disease and beta thalassemia. The focus is entirely on stock movements, M&A speculation, and Casgevy's commercial performance.
</Summary>


<Summary source="https://www.manilatimes.net/2026/03/03/tmt-newswire/globenewswire/prime-medicine-reports-full-year-2025-financial-results-and-provides-business-updates/2292160">
## Summary: Prime Medicine Financial Results & Business Update (March 3, 2026)

### Company Overview
Prime Medicine (Nasdaq: PRME) is a gene editing biotechnology company focused on "Prime Editing" technology, which they describe as a versatile, precise gene editing platform capable of correcting disease-causing mutations across a broad range of indications.

### Key Pipeline Updates Relevant to Potential Approvals

**PM 359 for Chronic Granulomatous Disease (CGD):**
- Most advanced program; ex vivo Prime Edited autologous HSC product
- Positive proof-of-concept data from first two patients in Phase 1/2 study announced in 2025
- Company believes data "supports an accelerated approval in the United States"
- **Currently in ongoing FDA engagement; plan to submit BLA (Biologics License Application) following final alignment with FDA**
- This is the only program near a potential approval filing

**Wilson Disease (WD) & Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency (AATD):**
- IND/CTA submissions planned for 1H 2026 and mid-2026, respectively
- Initial **clinical data** not expected until **2027** — far from approval stage

**Cystic Fibrosis:**
- Preclinical stage; proof-of-concept data anticipated in 2026

### Financial Status
- Cash runway into 2027 with $191M in cash, equivalents, investments, and restricted cash

### Relevance to Forecasting Question
The article indicates PM 359 for CGD is the only program potentially approaching regulatory approval in 2026, with a BLA submission pending final FDA alignment. No approval has been granted yet as of this press release date (March 3, 2026). All other programs remain in early clinical or preclinical stages.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://time.com/collections/time100-health-2026/7362503/john-evans/">
## Summary: TIME100 Health Feature on John Evans (Beam Therapeutics)

**Source:** TIME, February 11, 2026 | **Author:** Dominique Mosbergen

### Key Facts:

- **March 2025:** Beam Therapeutics announced results from an early-stage clinical trial of a gene-editing therapy for **alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD)**, a condition that can cause lung and liver damage.
- The trial involved **nine patients** who received a single infusion that corrected a genetic mutation via **base editing** (changing an incorrect DNA letter in the patient's body).
- CEO John Evans described this as **"the first time in history" a misspelled disease-causing gene was corrected** in a living patient.
- The correction is expected to be **permanent** (lasting the rest of patients' lives), potentially halting further lung and liver damage.
- **December (year implied ~2025):** Beam also reported preliminary success with a **separate base-editing therapy for sickle-cell disease**, showing restored red blood cell health in approximately **30 patients** in early trials.

### Stage of Development:
Both therapies are described as being in **early-stage (clinical trial) phases** — notably, **no regulatory approvals** are mentioned in this article for either therapy.

### Notable Quote:
Evans: *"There's been this dream in medicine to be able to work with DNA and have a genetic basis for cures. That dream is now tantalizingly close to being realized."*
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-026-04254-4">
## Summary: Phase 1 Trial of YOLT-101 Base Editing Gene Therapy for Familial Hypercholesterolemia

**Source:** Nature Medicine, published March 3, 2026. Trial sponsored by Yol Tech Therapeutics (a Chinese company); conducted at Ren Ji Hospital.

---

### What the Study Is

This is an interim report from an **ongoing Phase 1 clinical trial** (NCT06458010) evaluating YOLT-101, an investigational **in vivo adenine base-editing gene therapy** targeting heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HeFH) — a genetic disorder causing elevated LDL cholesterol and premature cardiovascular disease.

---

### Mechanism
YOLT-101 uses adenine base-editing technology delivered via **GalNAc-modified lipid nanoparticles** to inactivate the *PCSK9* gene in the liver, aiming for sustained LDL-C reduction.

---

### Key Results (6 participants, 3 men/3 women; escalating doses: 0.2, 0.4, 0.6 mg/kg)

- **No grade ≥3 adverse events** occurred
- Most common adverse events: transient infusion-related reactions and self-limited liver enzyme elevations
- At the highest dose (0.6 mg/kg, n=3) at **24 weeks**:
  - PCSK9 reduction: **74.4%**
  - LDL-C reduction: **52.3%**
- Reductions described as **dose-dependent and durable**

---

### Important Caveats
- This is a **Phase 1 trial** with only **6 participants** — very early-stage
- The trial is **ongoing**; these are interim results only
- The therapy is **not approved** by any regulatory body (FDA, MHRA, or EU Commission)
- The trial is conducted in **China**, not in the US, UK, or EU
- Several authors have financial conflicts of interest (cofounders/employees/advisors of Yol Tech Therapeutics)

---

### Relevance to Forecasting Question
This article describes promising early clinical data for a gene editing therapy targeting a **new condition** (HeFH, distinct from sickle cell disease/beta thalassemia). However, as a Phase 1 trial conducted in China with only 6 participants, it is **far from regulatory approval** by the FDA, MHRA, or EU Commission in 2026.
</Summary>

Query: Agenda and leaks for March 2026 European Medicines Agency CHMP meetings discussing any CRISPR or gene editing therapies for new disease indications
Here are the relevant news articles:

**REPROCELL Launches FDA-Compliant iPSC Master Cell Bank Manufacturing Service**
On March 3, 2026, REPROCELL (TYO : 4978), headquartered in Beltsville, Maryland, announced the launch of a Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP/BPF) compliant Master Cell Bank (MCB) manufacturing service for human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSC) for clinical use. According to the press release, this integrated workflow, supported by a commercialization grant (n° 2024-MSCRFCO-6418) from the Maryland Stem Cell Research Fund, spans from the production of iPSC StemRNA™ seeds and StemEdit genomic editing to the creation of FDA-compliant MCBs. The service aims to accelerate Investigational New Drug (IND) applications while reducing regulatory and manufacturing uncertainties for cell therapy developers. A key component is the StemRNA™ - LLF-34-F3 clinical seed clone, derived from a healthy American O+ female donor who provided full consent for commercial and therapeutic use; this clone is homozygous at HLA-A and HLA-DPA1 loci and is supported by an active FDA Drug Master File (DMF). The MCB is produced using closed-loop processes meeting FDA standards. For European alignment, REPROCELL offers MCB/WCB manufacturing via partner Histocell, which is GMP-compliant and authorized by the Spanish Agency of Medicines and Medical Devices (AEMPS) under the supervision of the European Medicines Agency (EMA). Dr. Chikafumi Yokoyama, CEO of REPROCELL Inc., stated that this unified platform is designed to 'accelerate IND/CTA submissions while preserving quality, traceability, and global regulatory alignment.'
Original language: fr
Publish date: March 03, 2026 02:00 PM
Source:[PR Newswire](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/reprocell-lance-la-fabrication-de-banques-de-cellules-maitresses-conformes-aux-bpf-pour-les-ipsc-cliniques-302700309.html)

**REPROCELL Launches GMP Master Cell Bank Service for Clinical iPSCs in Maryland**
On March 3, 2026, REPROCELL (TYO: 4978), based in Beltsville, Maryland, announced the launch of its Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP)-compliant Master Cell Bank (MCB) manufacturing service for human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This integrated workflow, spanning from clinical iPSC seed production and StemEdit gene editing to GMP MCB production, is designed to accelerate Investigational New Drug (IND) application submissions and reduce regulatory uncertainties for cell therapy developers. A key component of this platform is the StemRNA™ Clinical iPSC Seed Clone - LLF-34-F3, derived from a healthy female O+ US donor, which is homozygous at the HLA-A and HLA-DPA1 loci to enhance its potential for allogeneic applications. This clone is supported by an active FDA Drug Master File (DMF) and utilizes FDA-conformant closed system processes for GMP expansion. For sponsors seeking European alignment, REPROCELL offers MCB/WCB manufacturing through its partner Histocell, operating under a GMP certificate and approval from the Spanish Agency for Medicines and Medical Devices (AEMPS) under the supervision of the European Medicines Agency (EMA). Dr. Chikafumi Yokoyama, CEO of REPROCELL Inc., stated that the platform provides a streamlined path for clinical programs by coordinating donor screening, seed iPSC production, gene editing, and GMP cell banking. The initiative was supported by a commercialization grant (No. 2024-MSCRFCO-6418) from the Maryland Stem Cell Research Fund (MSCRF).
Original language: de
Publish date: March 03, 2026 02:00 PM
Source:[PR Newswire](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/reprocell-startet-gmp-master-cell-bank-produktion-fur-klinische-ipscs-302700342.html)

**EU Regulatory Reforms and Global Healthcare Innovation to be Focus of DIA Europe 2026**
DIA, a global non-profit organization of life science professionals, will host DIA Europe 2026 in Rotterdam, the Netherlands, from 24-26 March 2026, with the LIFT Start-Up Accelerator Programme running 23-24 March. The event aims to improve healthcare system efficiency, foster international collaboration, and discuss recent EU innovation and regulatory framework developments. According to the press release, the conference will feature over 300 speakers from more than 150 organizations, including senior representatives from the European Medicines Agency (EMA), the European Commission, national competent authorities, health technology assessment bodies, academia, patient organizations, and over 200 pharmaceutical and industry representatives. The programme includes over 100 sessions across 11 content tracks covering topics such as artificial intelligence, real-world evidence, digital transformation of clinical trials, European competitiveness, and reforms under the EU General Pharmaceutical Legislation. Pedro Barroca, DIA's Vice President & Managing Director for Europe, stated that 'the pace of innovation in healthcare and the unmet needs that drive it demand deeper alignment between all stakeholders involved in the development and regulation of new health technologies.' The Opening Plenary will feature Emer Cooke, Executive Director of the EMA, alongside representatives from the European Commission, the pharmaceutical industry, and patient organizations to outline a vision for Europe as a global leader in healthcare innovation. A high-level panel including Niklas Blomberg of the Innovative Health Initiative and María Lamas, Director of AEMPS and Chair of the HMA, will explore strategies to attract R&D investment, strengthen regulatory excellence, foster patient engagement, and reinforce Europe's role in shaping global standards. The signature DIAmond sessions, specifically the EU Regulatory Town Hall, will feature Emer Cooke joining François Houÿez (EURORDIS), Günter Waxenecker (AGES), Nathalie Moll (EFPIA), and Steffen Thirstrup (EMA) to discuss EU policy changes and implementation preparation. Additional sessions will feature speakers from Amgen, AstraZeneca, Bayer, GSK, MSD, Roche, Sanofi, and Novartis, focusing on global regulatory digitalization, sustainability standards, patient engagement, and advancing European healthcare initiatives. The event seeks to drive innovation, strengthen collaboration, and support inclusive and sustainable healthcare systems, with more information available at www.diaglobal.org.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 03, 2026 12:44 PM
Source:[Business Wire](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260302327466/en/EU-Regulatory-Reforms-and-Global-Healthcare-Innovation-to-be-Focus-of-DIA-Europe-2026)

**Intellia Therapeutics Presents Longer-Term Clinical Data for Lonvoguran Ziclumeran (lonvo-z); Hereditary Angioedema (HAE) Patient-Focused Research at AAAAI 2026 - Intellia Therapeutics (NASDAQ:NTLA)**
On March 03, 2026, in Cambridge, Mass., Intellia Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:NTLA) announced the presentation of four posters at the 2026 American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology Annual Meeting (AAAAI) held in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. The presentations focused on longer-term clinical data for lonvoguran ziclumeran (lonvo-z), an investigational in vivo CRISPR-based gene editing therapy. Lonvo-z is being evaluated in the HAELO Phase 3 clinical trial with the goal of becoming the first one-time treatment for hereditary angioedema (HAE) by inactivating the kallikrein B1 (KLKB1) gene. Interim Phase 1/2 clinical data demonstrated dramatic reductions in HAE attack rates and consistent, deep, and durable reductions in kallikrein levels. The therapy has received five regulatory designations: Orphan Drug and RMAT Designation from the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), Innovation Passport from the U.K. Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA), Priority Medicines (PRIME) Designation from the European Medicines Agency, and Orphan Drug Designation (ODD) from the European Commission. All posters are available on intelliatx.com.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 03, 2026 12:30 PM
Source:[Benzinga](https://www.benzinga.com/pressreleases/26/03/g50995702/intellia-therapeutics-presents-longer-term-clinical-data-for-lonvoguran-ziclumeran-lonvo-z-heredit)

**EMA approves 12 medicines including combined COVID-19 and influenza vaccine**
At its February 2026 meeting, the European Medicines Agency's (EMA) human medicines committee (CHMP) recommended the approval of 12 medicines, including vaccines, innovative treatments, and biosimilars, while issuing two negative opinions. A key highlight was the recommendation for mCombriax, the first combined mRNA vaccine designed to protect adults aged 50 and over against both COVID-19 and seasonal influenza. According to the EMA, this dual vaccine aims to reduce the burden of seasonal respiratory infections, given the context of hundreds of millions of COVID-19 cases reported in Europe and tens of millions of flu cases annually in the European Economic Area (EEA). Stéphane Bancel, Chief Executive Officer of Moderna, stated that the CHMP's positive opinion represents 'an important milestone for respiratory virus vaccination and for Moderna, with the introduction of the world's first flu plus COVID combination vaccine.' The decision was published by the EU Reporter on March 03, 2026.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 03, 2026 11:20 AM
Source:[EU Reporter](https://www.eureporter.co/health/2026/03/03/ema-approves-12-medicines-including-combined-covid-19-and-influenza-vaccine/)

**Acadia Pharmaceuticals Announces Plan to Request Re-Examination Following Negative CHMP Opinion for Trofinetide for the Treatment of Rett Syndrome**
Acadia Pharmaceuticals Inc. (Nasdaq: ACAD) announced on March 2, 2026, that the Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) of the European Medicines Agency (EMA) adopted a negative opinion regarding the Marketing Authorization Application for trofinetide to treat Rett syndrome in patients aged two years and older. Consequently, Acadia intends to request a re-examination of this opinion. The CHMP refusal was based on three perceived deficits: the treatment effect observed after 12 weeks was viewed as limited in magnitude; the LAVENDER trial did not capture all core symptoms of Rett syndrome; and the assessment of longer-term outcomes was influenced by patient discontinuations over time. Although the pivotal LAVENDER trial successfully met its co-primary and key secondary endpoints, the company notes these specific concerns. Catherine Owen Adams, Chief Executive Officer of Acadia, stated that while the company is 'disappointed by the CHMP's recommendation to refuse approval,' it remains 'encouraged by the meaningful benefits trofinetide has demonstrated for people living with Rett syndrome.' Markus Schulze, a caregiver and member of the Rett Syndrome Society Nordrhein-Westfalen from Germany, expressed hope that the therapy will be approved to help the EU Rett community. Trofinetide is currently approved in the United States, Canada, and Israel as the first and only treatment for Rett syndrome. Rett syndrome is a rare neurodevelopmental disorder occurring in approximately one of every 10,000 to 15,000 female births worldwide, typically caused by a genetic mutation on the MECP2 gene.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 02, 2026 09:05 PM
Source:[Business Wire](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260302383420/en/Acadia-Pharmaceuticals-Announces-Plan-to-Request-Re-Examination-Following-Negative-CHMP-Opinion-for-Trofinetide-for-the-Treatment-of-Rett-Syndrome)

**Sanofi (SNY) and Regeneron’s Dupixent Recommended for EU Approval**
On February 27, 2026, Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) announced that the European Medicines Agency's Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) adopted a positive opinion recommending the approval of Dupixent in the European Union to treat chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) in children aged two to 11 years. The recommendation applies to children with moderate-to-severe CSU who have had an inadequate response to histamine-1 antihistamines and are naïve to anti-immunoglobulin E therapy. Sanofi stated that data from the LIBERTY-CUPID clinical study program, including two phase 3 studies involving children aged six to 11 years and the single-arm CUPIDKids phase 3 study involving children aged two to 11 years, support this opinion. A final EU decision is anticipated in the coming months. Dupixent is already approved for CSU in adults and adolescents in Japan, the EU, and the US. Additionally, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has accepted a supplemental biologics license application for Dupixent in children aged two to 11 years, with a decision expected by April 2026. The article, published on March 02, 2026, by InsiderMonkey, notes that while Sanofi is a potential investment, the author believes certain AI stocks offer greater upside potential.
Original language: en-US
Publish date: March 02, 2026 08:45 PM
Source:[InsiderMonkey](https://www.insidermonkey.com/blog/sanofi-sny-and-regenerons-dupixent-recommended-for-eu-approval-1706972/)

**EMA Approves Single-Dose Acoziborol for Sleeping Sickness, Citing 96% Efficacy and 98% Case Reduction**
The European Medicines Agency (EMA) Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) has issued a positive opinion for acoziborol (Acoziborole Winthrop), a single-dose oral treatment for human African trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness), a disease transmitted by the infected tsetse fly that is almost always fatal without treatment. The approval covers early and advanced stages of the gambiense form in adults and adolescents aged 12 years or older weighing at least 40kg. Developed by the Drugs for Neglected Diseases initiative (DNDi) and Sanofi, this therapy replaces existing treatments that require 10 days of oral medication or a combination of injections and oral therapy, which often involved arsenic derivatives with severe side effects. Clinical data from a phase II/III study conducted in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and Guinea, published in 'The Lancet Infectious Diseases', demonstrated success rates of up to 96% at 18 months for both disease stages with a favorable safety profile. According to the article, the number of reported cases dropped from nearly 40,000 in 1998 to fewer than 600 in 2024, representing a 98% reduction since 2001. Dr. Luis Pizarro, Executive Director of DNDi, stated, 'In just 20 years, we have gone from complicated treatments including arsenic derivatives with serious side effects to today, when a single-day single-dose therapy could safely cure patients.' Dr. Erick Miaka, Director of the DRC's national sleep sickness control program, described the development as a 'victory for Africa-led science.' Audrey Duval, Executive Vice President of Corporate Affairs at Sanofi, noted the partnership has helped bring cases to 'historical lows' and aims to eliminate the disease by 2030. The EMA opinion supports regulatory approval in the DRC and lays the groundwork for WHO guideline updates to expand access in Central and West Africa.
Original language: es
Publish date: March 02, 2026 05:12 PM
Source:[La Razón](https://www.larazon.es/salud/europa-visto-bueno-terapia-mas-sencilla-enfermedad-sueno_2026030269a5bef32f00a0468831ac88.html)

**EMA CHMP Recommends First Oral Treatment for Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria**
The Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) of the European Medicines Agency (EMA) issued a positive opinion recommending the marketing authorization of remibrutinib, a new oral treatment developed by Novartis for adults with spontaneous chronic urticaria (UCE) who have an insufficient response to H1 antihistamines. According to Professor Martin Metz, Deputy Director of the Allergology Institute at Charité – Universitätsmedizin Berlin, 'the introduction of remibrutinib represents a significant advance, providing healthcare professionals with an oral treatment using a Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitor (BTKi), with symptom improvements observed from week 1.' The regulatory support targets a population that Metz describes as having 'an important unmet medical need,' as many patients cannot adequately control the disease with current treatments. Pivotal Phase III studies REMIX-1 and REMIX-2 demonstrated that remibrutinib improves itching and hives from week 1, with benefits sustained up to week 52, alongside early improvements in patient quality of life and sleep. The treatment was well-tolerated with no reported liver safety issues. Tonya Winders, President and CEO of the Global Allergy Patients Platform (GAAPP), stated that people living with UCE often face 'years of frustration and lack of recognition of their disease,' noting that unpredictable itching outbreaks 'can make it difficult to rest, concentrate on daily responsibilities, or even work normally at certain times.' She emphasized that an oral therapeutic option brings patients closer to 'recovering a full life.' Patrick Horber, President of Novartis International, declared that the CHMP's positive opinion 'represents an important step to address the significant unmet medical needs of adults living with UCE in Europe.' Remibrutinib would be the first targeted oral therapy for this condition, which affects approximately 4 million people in Europe, of whom more than 50% continue to have symptoms despite H1 antihistamine treatment. Horber added that Novartis will continue collaborating with regulatory authorities to ensure the drug 'is available for the patients who need it most.' Following the CHMP decision, the European Commission is expected to issue a final decision in approximately two months.
Original language: es
Publish date: March 02, 2026 11:50 AM
Source:[La Razón](https://www.larazon.es/sociedad/ema-luz-verde-primer-tratamiento-oral-urticaria-cronica-espontanea_2026030269a579792f00a04688313b5c.html)

**EU Panel Supports New Pediatric EoE Treatment**
On March 2, 2026, the European Medicines Agency's Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) issued a positive opinion recommending regulatory approval of Jorveza (budesonide, Dr. Falk Pharma GmbH) for pediatric patients with eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE). The new oral suspension formulation (0.2 mg/mL) is approved for children and adolescents aged 2-17 years, addressing a gap where clinicians previously relied on off-label adult treatments. According to the phase 2 PEER study involving 71 patients aged 2-18 years over 12 weeks, the medium-dose group achieved a 52.6% response rate and the high-dose group a 47.1% response rate, compared to 5.6% for placebo. Response was defined as a peak eosinophil count below 6 per high-power field and a more than 50% reduction in symptom scores. Histologic improvement was observed in more than 50% of the medium-dose group and 90% of the high-dose group. The medication demonstrated a favorable safety profile, with oral candidiasis reported in approximately 3.3% of participants and clinically insignificant small blood pressure increases, with no observed differences in growth velocity. The European Commission is expected to issue a final marketing authorization decision within 67 days of the CHMP opinion.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 02, 2026 12:00 AM
Source:[Medscape](https://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/eu-panel-supports-new-pediatric-eoe-treatment-2026a10006dp)

**EMA Recommends Approval of 12 New Medicines and Expands Indications for Six Others**
The European Medicines Agency's Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) recommended in its February 2026 meeting the approval of 12 new medicines and the expansion of therapeutic indications for six existing drugs. Among the new approvals are 'Ojemda' (tovorafenib) for pediatric low-grade glioma in patients six months and older, 'Onerji' (levodopa/carbidopa) for advanced Parkinson's disease, 'Palsonify' (paltusotina) for acromegaly, 'Rhapsido' (remibrutinib) for chronic spontaneous urticaria, 'mCombriax' (mRNA vaccine against influenza and COVID-19) for adults over 50, and 'Xolremdi' (mavorixafor) for WHIM syndrome, a rare inherited immune disorder. 'Acoziborole Winthrop' (acoziborol) received a positive opinion under exceptional circumstances for treating human African trypanosomiasis (sleeping sickness) caused by Trypanosoma brucei gambiense, with use limited to adults and adolescents aged 12 and above weighing at least 40 kg. The drug was evaluated under the EU-M4all program to improve access in low- and middle-income countries. The CHMP rejected 'Daybu' (trofinetide) for Rett syndrome, 'Iloperidona Vanda Pharmaceuticals' (iloperidona) for schizophrenia and bipolar mania, and withdrew the initial application for 'Zumrad' (sasanlimab) for bladder cancer. The CHMP also recommended expanded indications for six drugs, including 'Dupixent' (dupilumab) for children aged 2 to 11 with chronic spontaneous urticaria—the first biologic for this pediatric group—'Jorveza' (budesonide) for eosinophilic esophagitis in patients aged 2 to 17, and extensions for 'Keytruda', 'Olumiant', 'Scemblix', and 'Stelara'. The CHMP emphasized the need for equitable access to orphan medicines and increased research and prevention efforts for rare diseases.

**EMA Renewes Authorization for Gene Therapy Strimvelis for Rare Disease ADA-SCID**
The European Medicines Agency's (EMA) Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) has issued a positive opinion recommending the renewal of the marketing authorization for Strimvelis, a gene therapy for ADA-SCID, with unlimited validity. This decision comes ahead of the International Day for Rare Diseases. ADA-SCID, a rare genetic disorder preventing the development of a functional immune system, affects an estimated 6 to 11 newborns annually across the 27 EU member states. First approved in Europe in 2016, Strimvelis was developed based on research from the San Raffaele-Telethon Institute for Gene Therapy (SR-Tiget) in Milan and is among the first gene therapies ever made available as a medicine. It is currently the gene therapy with the longest track record on the market. Since 2023, the Telethon Foundation has taken full responsibility for the production and distribution of Strimvelis across the EU to prevent its withdrawal and ensure continuous patient access. Ilaria Villa, General Director of the Telethon Foundation, stated that maintaining the therapy's market availability is a 'remarkable scientific achievement' and a 'concrete demonstration' of the foundation's commitment to patients. She added that the foundation is proving 'a non-profit research and development model can ensure quality, continuity, and access to advanced therapies for rare diseases, even in the face of industrial disinvestment.'

**EU Opinion Extends Antifungal Coverage to Younger Patients**
At its January meeting, the European Medicines Agency's (EMA) Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) adopted a positive opinion recommending revisions to the approved indications for posaconazole (Noxafil, Merck). The changes extend pediatric use of posaconazole gastro-resistant tablets to treat invasive aspergillosis in children aged 2 years and older who weigh more than 40 kg, expand the use of posaconazole concentrate for solution for infusion to adults and pediatric patients aged 2 years and older for invasive aspergillosis, and allow posaconazole gastro-resistant powder and solvent for oral suspension to treat invasive fungal infections in pediatric patients aged 2 years and older. These updates align pediatric and adult eligibility across formulations. Conversely, broader pediatric use not restricted by formulation or clinical context has been removed, and treatment of rare invasive fungal infections—such as fusariosis, chromoblastomycosis, mycetoma, and coccidioidomycosis—is now limited to cases where disease is refractory to or patients are intolerant of standard antifungal therapies. Refractory disease is defined as progression or lack of improvement after at least 7 days of effective antifungal therapy. The CHMP opinion does not alter existing approvals for adult treatment of invasive fungal infections, prevention in high-risk patients, or oropharyngeal candidiasis. The changes are supported by pharmacokinetic bridging data showing comparable posaconazole exposure in children aged 2 years and older to effective and safe adult levels. The recommendation requires adoption by the European Commission before taking effect, after which updated prescribing guidance will be published in all EU official languages on the EMA website.

**2025 EMA Drug Approvals Outlook: A Year of Intentional, Biology Led Medicine Across Europe**
The European Medicines Agency (EMA) is expected to prioritize biology-first, precision medicine in 2025, with approvals emphasizing mutation-exact, genotype-defined, and severity-weighted therapies. This shift reflects a strategic move toward narrow indications, elevated focus on rare and severe diseases, and durability of clinical benefit over rapid market expansion. Convenience in dosing—particularly oral and subcutaneous administration—is increasingly integrated into benefit-risk assessments, linked to adherence, long-term outcomes, and healthcare system sustainability across neurology, cardiology, endocrinology, and chronic inflammatory diseases. Europe’s regulatory focus is transitioning from disease maintenance to disease interception, with early, mechanism-driven interventions in nephrology, pulmonology, rare genetic disorders, and immune-mediated diseases redefining clinical value through prevention and biological certainty. In oncology, approvals favor therapies targeting specific mutations (e.g., ROS1, EGFR) and resistance mechanisms, with drugs like Datroway (datopotamab deruxtecan), Augtyro (repotrectinib), Welireg (belzutifan), and Lazcluze (lazertinib) exemplifying this trend. Cardiology approvals emphasize disease-modifying treatments for conditions like transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy, prioritizing long-term functional preservation. Neurology sees rising regulatory traction for RNA-based therapies (e.g., antisense oligonucleotides) and infrequent subcutaneous dosing, balancing molecular specificity with real-world usability. In infectious diseases and critical care, host-directed therapies—such as monoclonal antibodies targeting inflammatory cascades—are gaining favor over pathogen-only strategies. Rare and genetic diseases remain central to EMA’s agenda, with approvals requiring clear genotype-phenotype alignment, functional endpoints, and early diagnosis relevance. Crosscutting modality trends include dominance of highly selective small molecules, the maturation of RNA-based therapies, and regulatory advantages for low-burden delivery systems. The overarching message is that precision medicine is now mandatory: biology defines approvability, convenience is clinical value, and rare, severe diseases justify premium regulatory attention. According to PharmaShots, 2025 marks a deliberate, intentional shift toward a biology-led regulatory framework in Europe.

**104 New Medicines Approved by EMA in 12 Months: Mapping Europe’s Medical Innovation Surge**
The European Medicines Agency (EMA) approved 104 new medicines within 12 months, marking the most intense year for pharmaceutical innovation in Europe over the past 15 years. Of these, 40% were biosimilars—highly similar biological drugs developed after patent expiration to offer more affordable alternatives—the highest number ever recorded. The Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) recommended 38 new active substances, 16 orphan drugs, 4 advanced therapy medicinal products (ATMP), 41 biosimilars, 10 generics, and 6 medicines under the PRIME program for therapies addressing unmet clinical needs. Steffen Thirstrup, Chief Medical Officer of the EMA, attributed the surge to delayed dossiers from the COVID-19 pandemic. Notable approvals include the first gene therapy for Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome, developed with support from Telethon, and a topical gene therapy gel for patients with dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa, a rare skin fragility disorder. Positive opinions were also granted for three medicines intended for use outside the EU, including an HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) drug. Oncology remains the most innovative field, with CAR-T therapies for refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, cord blood-based cell therapy for patients without compatible donors, and immunotherapy for high-risk non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer. Significant progress was also seen in endocrinology and metabolism, immunology, rheumatology, and transplantation, which ranked third in new active substances. Neurology saw advancements in early Alzheimer’s and Duchenne muscular dystrophy treatments. Cardiometabolic medicine—encompassing heart disease, diabetes, obesity, and metabolism—was highlighted as a key area where science, prevention, and regulation are converging rapidly.

**EMA Marketing Authorization of New Drugs in November 2025**
In November 2025, the European Medicines Agency (EMA) granted marketing authorization to eight new therapies in the EU, surpassing the five approvals issued by the U.S. FDA that month. The EMA’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) issued positive opinions for six therapies and the European Commission (EC) approved two. Sanofi’s Teizeild (teplizumab) received a positive CHMP opinion for delaying the onset of stage 3 type 1 diabetes in patients (≥8 years) with stage 2 disease, based on the P-II TN-10 trial, which showed a median delay of ~2 years and 57% of patients remained in stage 2 versus 28% on placebo. Ionis and Otsuka’s Dawnzera (donidalorsen) was recommended for routine prevention of hereditary angioedema (HAE) attacks in patients aged ≥12, supported by the P-III OASIS-HAE and OASISplus studies, including data on self-administration via autoinjector. Fondazione Telethon’s Waskyra, a single-dose ex vivo gene therapy for Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome (WAS), received a positive CHMP opinion; it uses lentiviral vector-modified CD34+ stem cells and is available at IRCCS Ospedale San Raffaele. BioNet’s VacPertagen, a recombinant acellular pertussis vaccine, was recommended as a booster for individuals ≥12 years and for maternal immunization to protect infants. Bayer’s Lynkuet (elinzanetant) received EC approval for moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms due to menopause or endocrine therapy for breast cancer, based on the P-III OASIS program across four studies, with favorable safety and efficacy outcomes published in The JAMA, JAMA Internal Medicine, and The NEJM. Insmed’s Brinsupri (brensocatib) was approved under the accelerated assessment pathway for non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis (NCFB) in patients ≥12 with ≥2 exacerbations in the prior year, supported by the P-III ASPEN trial (n=1,721), which showed a 19.4% reduction in annual exacerbations and delayed first exacerbation. Additionally, the CHMP recommended Inluriyo (imlunestrant) and GalenVita (germanium-68/gallium-68 chloride), though no press release was available for these. The approvals reflect sustained global momentum in advancing innovative treatments.

**CHMP Backs First Gene Therapy for Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome**
The European Medicines Agency's Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) has recommended granting a marketing authorization in the EU for Waskyra (etuvetidigene autotemcel), the first gene therapy for Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome (WAS), a rare genetic immunodeficiency almost exclusively affecting males. WAS results from mutations in the WAS gene, leading to impaired immune function, recurrent infections (including pneumonia and cytomegalovirus), eczema, easy bruising, bleeding, and increased cancer risk such as lymphoma. Waskyra is intended for patients aged 6 months or older who require hematopoietic stem cell transplantation but lack a suitable donor, addressing an unmet medical need. The therapy uses the patient’s own stem cells, genetically modified in the lab to produce functional WAS protein, and is administered as a single intravenous infusion. The recommendation is based on data from a clinical development program involving 27 patients: a single-arm trial in 10 children aged 1–9 years, supported by data from another trial and an expanded access program with 17 patients aged 1–35 years. Results showed a significant reduction in severe infections—from an annual rate of 2 prior to treatment to 0.15 events 1–2 years post-treatment and 0.12 events 2–3 years post-treatment—and a decline in moderate and severe bleeding episodes—from 2 events pre-treatment to 0.16 events 2–3 years post-treatment. Common side effects were related to the conditioning regimen, pretreatment, and administration site. The CHMP’s positive opinion will now be sent to the European Commission for an EU-wide marketing authorization decision. Waskyra was developed with support from an EMA pilot program for academic and nonprofit developers of advanced therapy medicinal products addressing unmet medical needs.

**EU Panel Denies New Active Status for Niemann-Pick Therapy**
The European Medicines Agency’s Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) denied new active substance status for levacetylleucine, the active ingredient in Aqneursa, following a reexamination requested by the manufacturer. The CHMP concluded that levacetylleucine could not be classified as a new active substance because it is not sufficiently distinct from previously authorized substances in terms of chemical structure or clinical benefit. This decision means Aqneursa will not receive the 10-year exclusivity period typically granted to new active substances, leaving it vulnerable to generic competition. Aqneursa is indicated for treating neurologic manifestations of Niemann-Pick type C (NPC) disease in adults and children aged 6 years or older and weighing at least 20 kg, either in combination with miglustat or as monotherapy in patients intolerant to miglustat. Levacetylleucine, a modified form of leucine, is believed to correct energy metabolism, particularly improving adenosine triphosphate production in cerebellar tissues, though its full mechanism of action remains unclear. The only adverse event causally linked to Aqneursa is flatulence. NPC is a rare, progressive, and fatal genetic disorder caused by mutations in lysosomal proteins essential for fat metabolism, leading to neurodegeneration and organ failure; most patients are children who die before age 20. There are currently no curative therapies for NPC. The decision was published on November 14, 2025, by Medscape.

**Sanofi, Insmed among winners of CHMP recommendations (SNY)**
During a recent meeting, the European Medicines Agency's (EMA) Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) issued positive recommendations for two new drugs: rilzabrutinib from Sanofi (NASDAQ:SNY) for immune thrombocytopenia and Brinsupri from Insmed (NASDAQ:INSM) for non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis. If approved by the European Commission (EC), Brinsupri would become the first therapy approved in the EU for this condition. Both drugs present new market opportunities. However, CHMP rejected Rezurock (from Sanofi) for chronic graft-versus-host disease despite its availability in other regions, prompting Sanofi to request a reexamination and delaying potential EU market access. Additionally, the CHMP endorsed new indications for drugs from Roche (Gazyva), Regeneron (Libtayo), Agios (Pyrukynd), Bristol-Myers Squibb, Pfizer, J&J, and Novartis, potentially expanding their market reach in the EU.

**Rare Diseases: Gene Editing Therapy exa-cel Now Reimbursable from Today**
The Italian Medicines Agency (AIFA) has published in the Official Gazette the reimbursement of exagamglogene autotemcel (exa-cel), the first and only gene-editing therapy approved in Europe, for patients with transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia (TDT) and severe sickle cell disease (SCD). Developed by Vertex Pharmaceuticals in collaboration with Crispr Therapeutics, exa-cel uses CRISPR/Cas9 technology to treat these genetic blood disorders. In Italy, approximately 5,000 individuals aged 12 and older live with TDT—the largest such population in Europe—and about 2,300 with SCD. Vertex Pharmaceuticals stated that the reimbursement approval marks a significant advancement in access to an innovative therapy that can transform patients' lives. Franco Locatelli, principal investigator of the Climb-111 and Climb-121 clinical trials and professor of Pediatrics at the Catholic University of the Sacred Heart in Rome, emphasized that clinical studies showed highly positive results and that exa-cel’s availability in Italy is a crucial milestone, significantly improving patients’ quality of life and addressing long-unmet medical needs. Federico Viganò, Country Manager for Italy and Greece at Vertex, highlighted that access to exa-cel represents a major achievement for patients with TDT and SCD, for whom therapeutic options were historically limited. He noted that a collaborative agreement with Italian health authorities recognizes the value of a one-time treatment with profound impact on patients, families, and the national healthcare system. The European Medicines Agency confirmed exa-cel’s efficacy in eliminating or reducing transfusions and vaso-occlusive crises in eligible patients in February 2024, leading to conditional marketing authorization by the European Commission.
